data.title: FDA staff cuts are already impacting regulatory work, Biotech leaders warn
data.description:
A group of biotech CEOs, investors, and patient advocates have urged Senate HELP Committee Chairman Bill Cassidy (R-LA) to intervene amid an ongoing restructuring program at the FDA which they said had impacted the agency’s day-to-day functions following massive layoffs.

 In March, the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) announced a massive restructuring program that involved the departure of as many as 10,000 full-time employees across its agencies, including 3,500 full-time staffers at the FDA.

Sending a letter to Cassidy, who has invited HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. to testify before the Senate HELP Committee on the matter, the group urged him to identify FDA functions impacted by the layoffs.

The companies interacting with the agency “have already encountered regulatory difficulties that we believe are the consequences of the FDA’s loss of experienced staff,” they said, adding that some of the regulator’s leadership roles remain vacant.

Patrizia Cavazzoni, who served as the FDA’s Director of the Center for Drug Evaluation and Research (CDER) joined as Pfizer’s (NYSE:PFE) Chief Medical Officer in February, while Peter Marks, the head of the Center for Biologics Evaluation and Research (CBER) was forced to resign in March.

“We worry that the institutional knowledge that makes the FDA the world’s leading regulatory body will be irretrievably lost due to the agency’s recent reduction in force and wave of retirements,” the group wrote.

“By maintaining deep agency expertise today, future reductions in force can be guided by those with the experience and vision necessary to build an efficient FDA that we and all Americans can count on.”

Following CEO were among signatories to the letter.  Paul Hastings of Nkarta (NKTX), Sarah Boyce, of Avidity Biosciences (NASDAQ:RNA), X4 Pharmaceuticals’ (XFOR) Paula Ragan, Arcutis Biotherapeutics’ (NASDAQ:ARQT) Frank Watanabe, Cedric Francois, Apellis (APLS), Beam’s (NASDAQ:BEAM) John Evans, Travere Therapeutics’ (TVTX) Eric Dube, Josh Mandel-Brehm of Camp4 Therapeutics (CAMP), PepGen’s (PEPG) James McArthur, Sutro Biopharma’s (STRO)

data.title: Study finds Seeking Alpha's small-cap coverage increases their acquisition likelihood
data.description:
Seeking Alpha's extensive reporting on small-cap companies increased their likelihood of being acquired due to enhanced visibility, according to a paper by Boston College and Harvard Business School authors.  

The paper, published in March, investigated whether social media coverage influenced a firm's likelihood of being acquired. The authors specifically looked at the impact on small-cap firms after increased coverage on Seeking Alpha.

Seeking Alpha is a financial research platform: https://about.seekingalpha.com/.

"We hypothesize that increased social media coverage of a low-visibility firm leads to greater awareness and understanding of the firm’s business by potential buyers, thereby increasing the likelihood of acquisition," the authors Pu Gu and Benjamin Yost from Boston College, and Yuan Zou from Harvard Business School, said.      

The authors wondered whether firms getting more coverage on Seeking Alpha saw more visibility among members of the investment community, thereby expanding the firm's investor base and grabbing the attention of potential acquirers. 

"Employing a novel setting in which Seeking Alpha contributors were incentivized to write articles about smaller firms, which historically had received relatively less coverage, we find evidence supporting our prediction," the authors concluded.

Seeking Alpha's coverage of small-cap firms (defined as companies with a market capitalization less than $1B) became extensive starting June 2013. The authors found that in the six quarters before and after the jump in coverage, small-cap firms exhibited a significant increase in acquisition likelihood. See the following two charts: 

"We also find the increase in Seeking Alpha coverage led to deals between acquirers and targets more geographically distant from each other and less likely to be in the same industry, consistent with a firm visibility channel," the study's authors said. 

Other academic research has also highlighted the impact of Seeking Alpha's investment insight and extensive coverage.

In February 2019, a study by Purdue University and University of Hong Kong authors found that Seeking Alpha's introduction of payments for contributing investor-analysts increased the amount of content output and generated more interest from the community.

A June 2021 paper by Texas A&M University, Brigham Young University, and Georgia Institute of Technology authors documented that sell-side coverage was influenced by content on Seeking Alpha.        

The March 2025 paper by Pu Gu, Benjamin Yost, and Yuan Zou is titled "Firm Visibility and Acquisition Likelihood: Evidence from Seeking Alpha Coverage" and can be accessed in the full here.

data.title: Brazil prepares new offshore oil auction to boost revenue as trade war threatens budget - Reuters
data.description:
Brazil's government is preparing an extra auction this year of stakes in offshore oil areas to raise revenue, a plan which is gaining steam due to falling oil prices and rising uncertainty from U.S. tariffs, Reuters reported Thursday.

The auction reportedly will involve smaller, uncontracted parts of the pre-salt Tupi, Mero and Atapu fields, looking to raise ~20B reais ($3.4B) in a "worst case" scenario.

The momentum for the auction reflects growing concerns about Brazil's fiscal outlook as President Trump's tariffs hurt global economic prospects, sending Brent crude prices to a four-year lows under $60/bbl earlier this week; even after rebounding, Brent closed Wednesday at $65.48/bbl, nearly 20% below the $80.79/bbl average price assumed in the country's 2025 budget law.

Brazil's Congress is expected to back the revenue-raising measure, according to the report, as it would provide a more comfortable margin for congressional earmarks at the center of tense budget negotiations.

Higher oil prices typically benefit Brazil, boosting revenue from royalties, corporate income taxes on oil producers, and hefty dividends from the Petrobras (NYSE:PBR) state-run oil company.

data.title: Consumers prices will be hit by 1pp, lowering US GDP by 0.3% - Pantheon
data.description:
President Donald Trump’s current tariff plans imply a 1 percentage point uplift to consumer prices, according to research by Pantheon Macroeconomics.

Although Trump has gone on and off on his tariff announcements and the markets have dealt with ongoing volatility – the S&P 500 (SP500) is lower on Thursday after seeing its best intraday gain since October 2008 – China’s 84% tariffs will lower U.S. GDP by 0.3%, and the economy will slow to a “near-standstill,” said Samuel Tombs, chief U.S. economist at Pantheon Macroeconomics.

“The 125% tariff on imports from China is so gargantuan and out-of-line with the rate applied to other countries that bilateral trade looks set to fall precipitously,” he said. “As a result, most of the impulse to consumer prices from the China tariffs will be indirect, via the switching of imports to other countries.”

Pantheon economists believe that 90% of imports from China will be rerouted elsewhere if the current tariff plan stays, implying an effective tariff rate on all U.S. imports of 17%.

Also, a 14 percentage point increase in the European Agreement Concerning the Work of Crews of Vehicles Engaged in International Road Transport (or AETR) would be consistent with a 1.4 percentage point increase to the core PCE deflator is costs are fully passed on to consumers, Tombs said.

“Retailers, however, probably will have to absorb some of the tariff cost, while the recent drop in oil prices and shipping costs has provided some offsetting relief too,” he said. “Accordingly, a 1 percentage point boost to consumer prices from the current menu of tariffs is a sensible base case.”

Economists previously forecasted a 1.25 percentage point increase to consumer prices immediately after Trump set out his plan on April 2. Later in the year, Pantheon foresees a peak of 3.25% increase to consumer prices.

In addition, Pantheon believes U.S. exports to China will fall to “near zero” by the third quarter if the current tariff plan remains in place, and that only a third of that impact will be offset by exports redirected to other markets.

“That implies total goods exports falling by 5%, reducing GDP by 0.35%,” Tombs concluded. “We are continuing to pencil-in quarter-on-quarter annualized growth of 0.5% in Q2 and 1.0% in Q3, though these remain ballpark estimates, subject to potentially big changes.”

More on U.S. economy:

  • March CPI Report: Inflation Cools, Tariff Risks Loom
  • KC Fed's Schmid stays focused on inflation outlook amid risks to growth
  • A Pleasant Surprise From U.S. Inflation, But It Won't Last Long
  • Core CPI inflation expected to tick up in March M/M, even before tariffs
  • Fed policymakers discussed increased risk of inflation at March meeting: FOMC minutes

data.title: Anglo says still in talks to sell Australia coal assets to Peabody following fire
data.description:
Anglo American (OTCQX:AAUKF) (OTCQX:NGLOY) said Thursday it continues to work with Peabody Energy (NYSE:BTU) to conclude the sale of its Australia coal mining operations for $3.78B, adding the Moranbah North coal mine is stable with no evidence of damage after a fire.

Anglo (OTCQX:AAUKF) (OTCQX:NGLOY) said it is making progress on the temporary suspension of mining operations at the Moranbah North mine following what it called a "minor ignition" in the underground area of the mine on March 31."

Moranbah is one of several steelmaking coal assets that Anglo (OTCQX:AAUKF) (OTCQX:NGLOY) plans to sell to Peabody (NYSE:BTU), whose shares surged Wednesday after the company said it was reviewing the deal because of the fire.

The company said it "continues to work with Peabody towards satisfying the remaining conditions in those agreements that are required for completion of the transaction," adding that conditions in the mine "remain stable, with data and camera footage showing no evidence of damage."

In July 2024, a fire at the prized Grosvenor mine - the largest in Anglo's (AAKUF) (OTCQX:NGLOY) coal portfolio, accounting for about a third of the company's production from the Australian coal fields - caused a long-term shutdown of operations; the Moranbah and Grosvenor mines are adjacent to each other.

If the Moranbah incident qualifies as a material adverse change in the agreement, Peabody (BTU) "could walk away or the two parties could revise the terms of the transactions," Jefferies analysts said.

data.title: Brookfield Asset Management upgraded at Bank of America on valuation, near-term catalysts
data.description:
REIT specializing in alternative asset management services, Brookfield Asset Management (NYSE:BAM) (TSX:BAM:CA), was upgraded at Bank of America on valuation support and near-term catalysts.

"In 1Q25 and 2Q25, we forecast back-to-back EPS beats, partly due to catch-up fees," said a team of analysts consisting of Craig Siegenthaler, Rodrigo Ferreira, Ivory Gao, and Eli Abboud.

"We remain positive on the multiple secular drivers supporting the Alts including GP consolidations, infrastructure, insurance, and private wealth," said the research note.

"In our view, the BAM stock is well positioned for these themes while offering a defensive FRE (fee-related earnings) rich profit stream that is currently being undervalued by the market and offers several near-term catalysts," said the note.

"Our $65 PO (price objective) implies a 42% total return putting BAM near the top of our diversified financials coverage; and its PEG ratio is attractive for a secular growth/defensive name at just 1x," said Bank of America.

The investment bank raised its recommendation on the stock to Buy from Neutral.

Bank of America's rating aligns with the Seeking Alpha authors rating of Buy. Meanwhile, the sell-side analysts and SA's Quant Rating system see BAM as Hold.

The stock was 0.63% down during Thursday morning trade to $46.73.

data.title: Additional U.S. tariffs on China to affect American film imports - Beijing
data.description:
China on Thursday said American film imports will be affected due to the additional reciprocal tariff against the Asian country by U.S. President Donald Trump.

In a public statement on the China Film Administration's website, the country's film regulator said, "The wrong move by the U.S. government to abuse tariffs on China will inevitably further reduce the domestic audience's favorability towards American films."

"We will follow market rules, respect the audience's choice, and moderately reduce the number of American films imported. China is the world's second-largest film market. We have always adhered to a high level of opening up to the outside world and will introduce more excellent films from the world to meet market demand," the spokesperson added when asked about the recent move by Trump and its impact on American film imports.

Trump on Wednesday said he was issuing a 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs for more than 75 countries that had reached out for negotiations but slapped China with an even higher rate.

He wrote on his social media platform, Truth Social, that "Based on the lack of respect that China has shown to the World’s Markets, I am hereby raising the Tariff charged to China by the United States of America to 125%, effective immediately."

data.title: Block to pay $40M to New York state over Cash App anti-money laundering failures
data.description:
Block (NYSE:XYZ) will pay a $40M penalty for failures in its Bank Secrecy Act/Anti-Money Laundering compliance program, the New York State Superintendent of Financial Services Adrienne A. Harris said on Thursday.

In addition to the penalty, Block (NYSE:XYZ) is required to hire an independent monitor to evaluate its compliance with the department's regulations and its remediation efforts.

The department's investigation found that Block's (XYZ) BSA/AML program had "critical gaps," including customer due diligence, failure to implement sufficient risk-based controls designed to prevent money laundering and illicit activity, and failure to effectively and timely monitor transaction.

Specifically, the company's tax treatment of high-risk bitcoin (BTC-USD) transactions allowed largely anonymous transactions to proceed without proper scrutiny, the NYDFS said.

"The rapid growth of Block’s Cash App absent a robust compliance function created risk and vulnerabilities that violated the rules financial services companies operating in New York must adhere to," Harris said.

Block (XYZ) stock dropped 3.0% in Thursday morning trading.
